title: Cat Rapid Breathing While Sleeping: Is It an Emergency? | Vet Triage
meta_desc: Learn why cats breathe fast while sleeping, when it is an emergency, and how to count your cat's resting respiratory rate for a vet triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat’s sleeping respiratory rate exceeds 40 breaths per minute, it is a veterinary emergency that may indicate heart failure or lung disease. Seek immediate care if you also notice panting or blue-tinted gums.

What does it mean if my cat is breathing rapidly while sleeping?

Rapid breathing, medically known as tachypnea, occurs when a cat's respiratory rate exceeds the normal resting range of 15 to 30 breaths per minute. When this happens during deep sleep—a time when the heart and lungs should be at their most relaxed—it is often a sign that the body is struggling to maintain proper oxygen levels or that there is fluid buildup in the chest.

Is rapid breathing in a sleeping cat a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. If your cat is consistently taking more than 40 breaths per minute while sleeping, this is a veterinary emergency.
  • Rapid breathing is frequently the first sign of serious conditions such as congestive heart failure, feline asthma, or pneumonia.
  • If you notice open-mouth breathing, panting, or blue-tinted gums, transport your cat to an emergency clinic immediately.

How does providing a photo or video of my cat's breathing help the veterinarian?

  • A clear video allows the medical team to observe the "effort" of the breath, noting if the cat is using abdominal muscles or if breaths are shallow and fast.
  • Cats often change their breathing patterns due to the stress of a clinic visit, so home footage provides more accurate diagnostic data.
  • Capturing the behavior in a relaxed environment can be life-saving for accurate triage and diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in cats manifests as an increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by a change in respiratory pattern. Cats may sit crouched on all four limbs with the sternum slightly elevated. Obvious labored, open-mouth breathing and changes in mucous membrane color (gray and/or blue [cyanosis]) develop last and indicate significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. If pulmonary edema is suspected in a cat that is not in critical condition, monitor the sleeping respiratory rate (SRR) in a cool environment. If pulmonary edema is present, the SRR will be increased.

Chapter: Cardiology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 113)

faqs.question: How do I accurately count my cat's respiratory rate?
faqs.answer: Wait until your cat is fast asleep. Watch the rise and fall of the chest; one rise and fall counts as one breath. Count the number of breaths in 30 seconds and multiply by two to get the breaths per minute.

faqs.question: Could my cat just be having a bad dream?
faqs.answer: While cats may twitch or have brief, irregular breaths during REM sleep, the breathing rate should return to normal quickly. Persistent rapid breathing that lasts several minutes is not a dream and requires medical attention.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of rapid breathing in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include fluid in the lungs (pulmonary edema), fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion), heart disease, asthma, or severe anemia.
